ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for red-braised beef noodle soup; closest_parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest available category; no specific code exists for red-braised beef noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for braised beef noodle soup as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of braised beef noodle soup weighing approximately 500 g, comprising noodles, braised beef cuts, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef noodle soup, Taiwanese-style — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef, shank (cooked, simmered) [ID 13352]; Noodles, egg, cooked [ID 20113]; Soup, beef broth/bouillon, ready-to-serve [ID 06159]; and condiment components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for red-braised beef noodle soup as a complete dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked beef shank (~30% by weight), cooked wheat noodles (~25% by weight), beef broth (~40% by weight), and minor condiment/vegetable components (~5% by weight). Proportions are based on standard Taiwanese beef noodle soup recipe ratios documented in culinary literature. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and bean paste in the braising liquid.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles (boiled) GI ~50 used as analogous estimate for the noodle component; whole-dish GL calculated from carbohydrate_g per serving
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for red-braised beef noodle soup as a complete dish. GI of 50 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~50, low category), which constitutes the primary glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The protein and fat content of the beef and broth components further attenuate the postprandial glycemic response, making GI 50 a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 52.5 g) / 100 = 26.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, bean paste, and beef broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit portion size, and avoid drinking the soup base.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles partially offset by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GL of 26 per bowl is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the noodle portion, substituting with lower-GI konjac or shirataki noodles if available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef shank and braising liquid
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2100 mg per bowl) and saturated fat (~6 g per bowl) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner beef cuts, requesting less braising sauce, and limiting frequency of consumption are recommended strategies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red-braised beef noodle soup (红烧牛肉汤面, Hóng Shāo Niúròu Tāng Miàn) is a celebrated Taiwanese noodle dish in which wheat noodles are served in a deeply flavoured, soy-based braised beef broth alongside tender slow-cooked beef cuts, typically beef shank or brisket. The braising liquid is built from soy sauce, rice wine, doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), ginger, garlic, star anise, and dried chillies, producing a rich, umami-forward, mildly spicy broth with a characteristic reddish-brown colour. The beef is braised for several hours until fork-tender, yielding a melt-in-the-mouth texture that contrasts with the chewy, springy wheat noodles. The dish is commonly garnished with sliced scallions, pickled mustard greens, and chilli oil. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g restaurant bowl provides approximately 490 kcal, 39 g protein, 16 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, moderate-energy meal. Sodium content is high (~2100 mg per bowl) due to the soy-based braising liquid, which is the primary clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the wheat noodle component is approximately 50 (low category), and the co-ingestion of protein and fat from the beef further moderates the postprandial glycemic response, making it a moderate glycemic load meal for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a staple of Taiwanese cuisine and is widely served across Chinese restaurants in Singapore, including at Din Tai Fung. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or soy.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA) contains a complete nutrient profile for red-braised beef noodle soup as a prepared dish. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for the constituent components (beef shank, wheat noodles, beef broth, condiments) using standard recipe proportions. GI is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles as the closest analogous carbohydrate source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single approved source entry exists for this complete dish. 2. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100 g) reflects the soy sauce and bean paste-heavy braising liquid typical of red-braised preparations; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant and recipe. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the complete dish was found in approved sources. 6. GL per serving calculated as (50 × 52.5 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 26.3. 7. The dish is not halal-certified at Din Tai Fung Singapore as of the accessed date; is_halal is set to false. 8. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects minor naturally occurring trans fats from beef; not industrially added. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific dish in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
